items.rationale: His headline 2024 thesis: established brands will use NFTs to represent customer identity, bridge physical/digital goods, and co-create with enthusiasts. Core argument of his forthcoming book 'The Everything Token.' Highest-conviction, most-restated view.
items.age_note: Built steadily through 2022-23; peaks in Dec 2023 with the a16z trend prediction and book launch.

citations.quote: NFTs are poised to become ubiquitous brand assets in 2024.
brands can do so much more: They can use NFTs to represent and reinforce customer identity and community affiliations

items.rationale: What originally drew him in: NFTs create and verify ownership of digital assets independent of any platform, defining property rights and exchange to enable entirely new markets (tickets, game items, media).

citations.quote: NFTs provide a way of creating and verifying ownership over digital assets... independent of any specific platform.
they're a tool for making markets

items.rationale: Sees crypto as a live laboratory for incentive design. Champions wave mints (on-chain reputation as proof-of-personhood, avoids gas wars/bots) and clearing-price over pay-as-bid auctions. This is his personal scholarly edge applied to the space.

citations.quote: the wave mint format means that people's wallets effectively function as a form of 'proof of unique personhood.'
I generally prefer clearing-price auctions to descending pay-as-bid auctions

items.rationale: Repeatedly argues follow-graphs and reputation should be portable and interoperable à la web3; the friction of migrating off Twitter to Threads is his proof-point that closed platforms trap users.

citations.quote: This shows why we need social media based on open protocols
ideally this data would be portable and interoperable, à la web3.

items.rationale: His own economic-theory paper (with Hatfield): public on-chain transaction history lets entrants directly recruit incumbents' loyal customers, restoring competition and driving consumer prices down. Genuinely excited, treats it as a virtuous dynamic.

citations.quote: vampire attacks may contribute to increased – and hopefully virtuous – platform competition.
Vampire attacks restore competition for loyal customers.

items.rationale: Believes startups can launch centralized and decentralize over time via a 'decentralization equalizer'; firms architected for eventual decentralization will win. Designing for it upfront is a moat.

citations.quote: the one that's better set up for it upfront is likely to be the winner.
Progressive decentralization is possible

items.rationale: Reads FTX/Celsius/Terra collapses not as crypto's death but as vindication of decentralized finance; explicitly contrasts DeFi's transparency and aligned incentives against centralized intermediaries' risk-taking.

citations.quote: Do the collapses of FTX, Celsius, and Terra signal the end of crypto? No, but they do show why we need #DeFi.

items.rationale: Views on-chain games as the ideal setting and laboratory for incentive/mechanism design; actively advises and plays (ProofOfPlay/Pirate Nation, Applied Primate/MegaForce Sentinels).

citations.quote: On-chain games are a perfect setting (and laboratory) for incentive design

items.rationale: Long-standing thesis that cc0/open licensing jumpstarts derivative ecosystems and turns IP into composable culture; extended into excitement about web3-native IP infrastructure (Story Protocol).
items.age_note: Originated Aug 2022 (cc0 article); restated and broadened through 2023 via book work and Sept 2023 Story Protocol engagement, so still current.

citations.quote: cc0 allows this to happen with the explicit permission of the original creators – thus giving an NFT's enthusiast community a literal license to build
fascinated by the potential of web3 to transform the way we understand, create, and manage IP.

items.rationale: His own caveat to the vampire-attack thesis: induced competition only helps consumers if it doesn't push firms into malbehavior, citing CeFi platforms taking on excess risk. The main downside he flags in his own work.

citations.quote: can only benefit consumers if it doesn't drive firms to engage in malbehavior (such as with centralized crypto finance platforms taking on excess risk).

items.rationale: Flags that current vampire-attack/competition models may be unsustainable because they lean on regulatorily suspect strategies; amplifies calls for well-calibrated crypto legislation.

citations.quote: it's not clear how sustainable the current vampire attack models are, especially since many of them rely on strategies that are regulatorily suspect.

items.rationale: Recurring practitioner concern that badly-designed sales create supply/demand imbalance and destructive gas wars; publicly critiqued Azuki's Elementals mint for abandoning a stealth-airdropped mint-pass that would have prevented one.

citations.quote: fundamentally gas wars are about supply/demand (im)balance
there may instead be significant demand/supply imbalance in the sale -- which would lead to... a 'gas war'

items.rationale: Persistently warns about compromised accounts, impersonation scams, and the security exposure of having users mint from wallets holding other high-value assets. Treats it as an ongoing operational risk for the space.

citations.quote: there are security issues around having people mint for wallets that all other, potentially high-value digital assets.
the former WhiteHouseCEA Chair's account appears to have been hacked and is currently being used to share scam links

items.rationale: Argues the realistic, high-probability AI risk isn't existential but mundane data exposure — personal identifiers leaked via models trained without consent on private files (e.g., Dropbox defaulting AI-sharing on). Adjacent to, not strictly, crypto.

citations.quote: people's personal identifiers will be leaked via generative models that were trained -- without their knowledge -- on tax files stored in their Dropboxes.

items.rationale: Deeply embedded as a '532' contributor; co-authored the a16z write-up of its 'wave mint' as a replicable distribution mechanism drawing on established cc0 networks. Excitement is about the model/community, not price.

citations.quote: Overall we found the wave mint approach a 1337 solution for the 1337skulls drop.

items.rationale: Cites it as the exemplar proving his brand-NFT thesis: a gamified loyalty program distributing collectible digital assets to mainstream consumers.

citations.quote: Starbucks... has introduced a gamified loyalty program in which participants collect digital assets as they explore the company's coffee offerings

items.rationale: Concern is mechanism-specific, not a price call: the mint abandoned a stealth-airdropped soulbound mint-pass, risking a supply/demand imbalance and gas war. He publicly offered to advise on market design.

citations.quote: moderators in the Discord are saying the SBT description is incorrect... of which there are substantially more than available Elemental supply.

risk_regime.rationale: Structurally bullish on crypto/web3 as a builder and researcher, not as a speculator. Dismisses 'is crypto dead' narratives post-FTX, analogizing the moment to the internet circa 1997 (pre-Google), and frames the sector's problems (CeFi blowups, regulation, gas wars, scams) as solvable design challenges rather than reasons to retreat. His optimism is durable and thesis-driven; risk-awareness shows up as mechanism critiques, not as bearishness on the asset class.

notes: Kominers is an a16z crypto researcher and market-design economist, so his 'market view' is overwhelmingly about mechanisms, adoption, and brand strategy rather than token prices — he expresses essentially no directional investment views on fungible tokens, and his enthusiasm for specific NFT collections (1337skulls, Oldeus, Divine Anarchy, Forgotten Runes, etc.) reads as collector/community participation, not market calls. Token arrays are therefore deliberately sparse and caveated; treat them as 'projects/mechanisms he admires' not 'longs.' Recency weighting strongly favors the brand-NFT thesis, which sharpened into an explicit 2024 prediction in Dec 2023. No major reversals found over the 18-month corpus; the cc0 thesis (2022) persisted and broadened rather than reversing. His most-repeated CONCERN by volume is actually U.S. COVID/public-health policy (mitigation, long COVID, vaccine logistics) — but that is a civic/policy stance, not a market view, so it is excluded here. Adjacent-to-crypto AI privacy concern is included because he frames it as a near-term technology-market risk he holds with conviction.
